[JS Weird World] No.22 Objects and the Dot Operator
JsWeirdWorld
JavaScript 全攻略:克服JS奇怪的部分

We're starting a new chapter: objects and functions. A lot of courses teach these two as separate topics, and honestly that's a huge mistake. In most languages they really are separate things, but in JavaScript they're deeply connected. In this post we look at objects from a fresh angle and dig into how the dot operator actually works under the hood.

[JS Weird World] No.21 A Framework Reminder: Default Values

Time for a little conceptual reminder about frameworks. This time the course uses jQuery as the example. Why jQuery? Because back in the day it was the most popular framework around — animations, browser compatibility, you name it, jQuery handled all of it. So it's the perfect thing to use as an example here.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/17/js-weird-world-20-default-values/
[JS Weird World] No.20 Default Values
JavaScript Full Guide: Conquering the Weird Parts of JS

Last time we dug into some of the quirks of coercion. This time we're going to flip it around and put those quirks to work — starting with default values. What happens when you call a function without passing the argument it expects? And how do you set a default so it doesn't blow up? Let's find out.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/17/js-weird-world-19-existence-and-booleans/
[JS Weird World] No.19 Existence and Boolean Values

Normally we can manually use functions like Boolean and Number to force a type conversion, but the course also stresses you shouldn't do that (manual conversion) — it's fine for demos though. Here we convert undefined into a boolean and see what comes out, then use that behavior to check whether data exists.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/16/js-weird-world-18-comparison-operators/
[JS Weird World] No.18 Comparison Operators: Why Is 3 < 2 < 1 Actually true?

Comparison operators look simple until you hit something like 3 < 2 < 1 and JavaScript hands you back true. In this post we walk through operator associativity, type coercion, NaN, and why == and === aren't the same thing — and why you should basically always reach for ===.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/16/jsweirdworld-17-coercion/
[JS Weird World] No.17 A Quick Reminder: Type Coercion

What does coercion actually mean? It's when JavaScript quietly turns one type into another — like a String into a Boolean. It happens all over the place in JS, and in this post we'll look at a simple example of how it shows up when you do math on two different types.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/16/js-weird-world-16-operator-precedence-associativity/
[JS Weird World] No.16 Operator Precedence and Associativity

Operator precedence and associativity are two really important concepts in JavaScript. They sound dry, but once you actually get them, they help a ton when you're debugging. This post breaks down what each one means, why they matter when two operators share the same precedence, and how to read MDN's precedence table.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/16/jsweirdworld-15-operators/
[JS Weird World] No.15 Quick Reminder: Operators Are Secretly Functions

An operator is basically a special method — you could even call it a function — that JavaScript hands you to do calculations. So how do you actually use one? This post walks through the plus operator, comparison operators, and the slightly mind-bending idea that all of them are really just functions under the hood.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/16/js-weird-world-14-primitive-values/
[JS Weird World] No.14 Primitive Values: The Six Types JavaScript Actually Has

We've already done a quick intro to JavaScript's types. This chapter goes deeper into JavaScript's dynamic typing — what it is, and what its pros and cons are. Along the way we'll walk through the six primitive types: Undefined, Null, Boolean, Number, String, and Symbol.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/16/jsweirdworld-13-types-and-javascript/
[JS Weird World] No.13 Quick Concept Check — Types and JavaScript

This kicks off a brand-new chapter: "Types and Operators." We start with how JavaScript handles types — dynamic typing, where you don't have to tell JS what type a variable is because it figures that out for you. We'll use typeof to peek at string, number, and boolean values, and then contrast it with static typing in languages like C#.
